romania.title: Un copil de 7 ani a fost luat pe sus de tată dintr-o școală din Sălaj, după o neînțelegere cu fosta soție
romania.summary: Au fost momente tensionate într-o școală din Sălaj: un bărbat de 59 de ani, cetățean italian, a intrat într-o sală de clasă pentru a-și vedea fiul în vârstă de 7 ani. Potrivit martorilor, acesta l-ar fi luat în brațe pe copil și ar fi ieșit în grabă din clasă, provocând panică în rândul profesorilor.

medical.title: High blood pressure associated with lower risk of dementia in frail people
medical.summary: For people with physical frailty, having high blood pressure may be associated with a lower risk of dementia, according to a study published in Neurology. The study did not find a lower risk of dementia in people with high blood pressure who were more robust. High blood pressure was associated with a higher risk of dementia in people with no signs of frailty.

medical.title: Fewer than 1 in 4 patients with stroke and 1 in 7 with brain injury receive inpatient rehab, finds study
medical.summary: Fewer than one in four people with stroke and fewer than one in seven people with traumatic brain injury receive inpatient rehabilitation care after being hospitalized, according to a study published in Neurology Open Access. Researchers found disparities in access across sex, race, insurance coverage and income level.

medical.title: Hidden muscle resistance in advanced dementia is involuntary, not refusal, new papers explain
medical.summary: Baycrest researchers and clinicians have contributed to newly published research advancing understanding of paratonia, a common but often underrecognized condition affecting people living with advanced dementia.

medical.title: Sleep and exercise may curb heart risk from mutant white blood cells
medical.summary: Healthy sleep and regular exercise can work to counteract genetic mutations in white blood cells that are associated with cardiovascular disease and are most common among older people, Mount Sinai researchers have found. In a study published in Nature, the team reported for the first time that sufficient sleep and exercise can help reduce the cancer-like cell expansion and atherosclerotic risk linked to mutations that spontaneously occur in white blood cells.

medical.title: Think exercise can undo the effects of sitting all day? You may want to stand for this, study suggests
medical.summary: A new Dalhousie study suggests improved fitness may not be enough to protect blood vessels from the effects of prolonged sitting. The study, published in the European Journal of Applied Physiology, examined whether 12 weeks of high-intensity interval training could protect blood-vessel function in the leg after two hours of uninterrupted sitting.

medical.title: Bilingual brains keep concepts aligned across languages, individual neuron data suggest
medical.summary: Researchers at Baylor College of Medicine have uncovered a fundamental principle underlying how the human brain processes meaning across multiple languages. In a new study posted to the bioRxiv preprint server, scientists recorded the activity of individual neurons in the human hippocampus and found that bilingual people appear to organize concepts in a shared neural structure—even when speaking entirely different languages.

medical.title: Can virtual reality train surgeons? A 2,000-year-old experiment reveals what's missing
medical.summary: As medical schools increasingly turn to virtual reality, 3D models and digital simulations, a new correspondence in Nature Medicine argues that one essential part of clinical training remains difficult to digitize: the hands-on judgment physicians develop through real-life, mentored practice.

medical.title: Sleep gets price tag in new survey of 455 US adults
medical.summary: A new study to be presented at the SLEEP 2026 annual meeting found that people place different financial values on sleep, with those differences linked to factors such as age, income and existing attitudes about sleep.

medical.title: ACS Geriatric Surgery Verification hospitals screen nearly all older surgical patients for delirium, study finds
medical.summary: There is a stark gap in how often hospitals screen older adults for postoperative delirium, with verified geriatric surgery programs screening nearly every patient while nonaccredited programs screen just half, according to new findings published in the Journal of the American College of Surgeons. This disparity may mean thousands of cases are going undetected and unaddressed.

medical.title: Expecting poor sleep is associated with worse postpartum sleep quality
medical.summary: A new study to be presented at the SLEEP 2026 annual meeting found that pregnant women's expectations about postpartum sleep predicted their actual sleep quality after delivery, outweighing prior sleep and psychiatric history.

medical.title: Early Rett syndrome clues emerge as 12 genes shift before symptoms appear
medical.summary: To better understand what drives the emergence of symptoms in Rett syndrome, researchers at Baylor College of Medicine and the Duncan Neurological Research Institute (Duncan NRI) at Texas Children's Hospital took a closer look at brain cells in mice modeling Rett syndrome before symptoms appeared. They identified a set of dysfunctional genes and specific cell types that are vulnerable early to genetic changes. The study appears in Science Advances.

medical.title: Ultrasensitive blood test predicts head and neck cancer relapse months earlier
medical.summary: A new study by investigators from Mass General Brigham Cancer Institute has found that an ultrasensitive blood test called HPV-DeepSeek could help identify which people with HPV-associated head and neck cancer still had cancer cells in their bodies after surgery and may benefit the most from additional treatments. The results are published in Science Translational Medicine.

science.title: How anti-CRISPR proteins promote the spread of hospital-acquired infections
science.summary: Researchers from Skoltech—a VEB.RF group institution—and their colleagues from the U.S. and China have explained how the antibiotic resistance gene established itself in the genome of the bacterium Klebsiella pneumoniae. The findings could help control this widespread microbe, which can cause pneumonia, meningitis and other, often hospital-acquired, infections in patients with weakened immunity. The findings were reported in the Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences.

science.title: Report: ICE surges have triggered massive job losses—including among Americans
science.summary: Since January 2025, the Trump administration has ramped up immigration enforcement. A key rationale for the policy is that it will open up jobs for Americans by reducing competition from undocumented immigrants.

science.title: AI model 'hears' Bryde's whale calls in seismic data from South China Sea
science.summary: Researchers have repurposed an AI model designed for visual identification tasks to detect Bryde's whale calls contained within seismic data collected in the South China Sea. The detection system precisely identified calls more than 96% of the time and even identified some call events that were missed when the data were examined by hand, according to the study published in Seismological Research Letters.

science.title: Everyone wants to think they're open‑minded. Here's why most people aren't
science.summary: Most people think they are open-minded and would like others to perceive them as such. But for the things that matter most—religious beliefs, for example, or the meaning of life—few of us are genuinely willing to consider that we might be wrong, let alone do the hard work of revising beliefs. At a fundamental level, we humans are somewhat closed-minded.

science.title: How ice-age sea-level falls may have turned seafloor volcanoes into ocean fertilizer
science.summary: Ice-age sea-level declines may have turned seafloor volcanoes into natural iron fertilizer for plankton, potentially enhancing ocean carbon storage, Boston College researchers report in the journal Nature Geoscience.

science.title: 'Cool Routes' finds cooler walking paths with hourly forecasts and street-level shade data
science.summary: The Arizona sunshine hits like a blowtorch. The pavement radiates heat like a stove burner. To make hot-weather walking less of an ordeal, Arizona State University researchers have created a web-based app that finds the coolest, shadiest ways to reach destinations on foot.

science.title: Dads today talk more freely with their teens about sex and relationships
science.summary: For many dads, talking with their teenager about sex and relationships can feel like a minefield.

science.title: The Milky Way was rewired by a cataclysmic collision billions of years ago. Now it is on course for another
science.summary: No matter the time or vantage point, from a pre-Neolithic cave to a post-lockdown London high-rise, the predictability of the night sky has always been humanity's symbol of permanence and reassuring stability.

science.title: 'Black hole stars'—Webb finds strongest evidence yet
science.summary: The complex puzzle known as little red dots has become more complete since their initial discovery by NASA's James Webb Space Telescope in 2022. Now a particular little red dot's spectrum is helping connect many of the pieces.

science.title: Farmers in a national park are turning down lights at night to help wildlife. It could be good for crops too
science.summary: Growing evidence suggests that excessive outdoor lighting at night may be harming wildlife.

science.title: 'Atmospheric scrubbing' could reduce cooling effects of stratospheric aerosol injections
science.summary: The quest to identify a new way to potentially counter one of the world's most widely discussed solar geoengineering proposals has taken a new, exciting turn—raising questions about how future climate interventions could be governed.

science.title: DNA tetrahedrons unlock sharper cancer targeting with vitamin E tweak
science.summary: Conventional cancer treatments, such as chemotherapy, often lack specificity and can damage both cancerous and healthy cells, leading to severe side effects. With this in mind, researchers at Indian Institute of Technology Gandhinagar (IITGN) have developed DNA nanostructures called tetrahedrons and modified them by attaching a vitamin E-derived molecule called alpha-tocopherol succinate (αT), which can disrupt vital functions inside cancer cells while acting protectively in healthy cells. By incorporating αT into the DNA tetrahedrons, the researchers significantly enhanced cellular uptake and improved anticancer efficacy, resulting in more selective and effective elimination of cancer cells.

science.title: NASA, NOAA to Hold Joint Session at 23rd Symposium on Operational Environmental Satellite Systems
science.summary: Abstracts are now being accepted for the session, which will take place at the 2027 AMS Annual Meeting.

science.title: More sustainable agriculture: Recycled fertilizers could be part of the solution
science.summary: Researchers have uncovered new insights into how phosphorus from recycled materials moves through soil—offering guidance to support more sustainable fertilizer use. Phosphorus is an essential nutrient for plant growth, yet many soils lack enough of it to support strong crop yields. Farmers often rely on fertilizers made from mined phosphorus, a limited, nonrenewable resource.

environment.title: Experts say ‘bare bones’ US laws are unfit to regulate nascent deep-sea mining industry
environment.summary: This is part 2 of a two-part series examining the U.S.’s efforts to begin deep-sea mining in federal waters. Part 2 examines the regulations that would govern the industry. Part 1 explored the process behind proposed lease sales in U.S. federal waters and reactions to those plans. The deep-sea mining industry could launch in the […]

environment.title: Colombia passes landmark cattle traceability law to combat illegal deforestation
environment.summary: Colombia passed a landmark law June 4 aimed at improving traceability of its cattle supply chain to ensure beef isn’t sourced from deforested land. The law hopes to enhance existing traceability systems and make it easier to identify when cattle have grazed in protected areas and forests that were illegally cleared for pasture. “This is […]

environment.title: Kenya’s former Chief Justice David Maraga arrested at protest of national park construction
environment.summary: NAIROBI, Kenya (AP) — Kenya’s former Chief Justice David Maraga said he was arrested Monday alongside other activists protesting planned construction inside Nairobi National Park. Police fired tear gas canisters at the protesters who were marching outside the park while carrying banners with messages denouncing land grabs. Maraga was detained and later released while staging […]

environment.title: Urban wildlife is changing from the inside out (commentary)
environment.summary: Cities are expanding faster than at any point in human history, and wildlife is adapting in remarkable ways. We often talk about visible changes like animals becoming bolder, shifting their diets, or altering their daily rhythms to avoid people. But there is a deeper transformation happening inside their bodies, one that conservation science has barely […]

environment.title: Scientists discover vast hidden structure beneath Antarctica’s ice
environment.summary: A giant fan-shaped network of hidden basins has been discovered beneath East Antarctica, revealing that several well-known subglacial features are actually part of one massive geological structure. The finding sheds new light on Antarctica’s ancient tectonic history and could help scientists better understand how the ice sheet behaves today.

environment.title: This prehistoric fish may explain how animals first walked on Earth
environment.summary: Scientists have peered inside the skull of a 380-million-year-old Antarctic fish that was closely related to the first animals to walk on land, revealing surprising clues about how life began its move out of the water. Using advanced neutron imaging, researchers discovered that Koharalepis jarviki had features suited for living near the water’s surface, including openings in its skull that may have helped it gulp air and a light-sensitive organ linked to day-night rhythms.

environment.title: 100-million-year-old bug had crab-like claws unlike any known insect
environment.summary: Deep inside 100-million-year-old amber from Myanmar, scientists uncovered a bizarre ancient bug with clawed front legs that look more like a crab’s pincers than anything seen in modern insects. The discovery is so unusual that researchers say these crab-like “chelae” evolved independently in this lineage, making it only the fourth known example of such structures appearing in insects at all.

environment.title: Scientists discover why some DNA-doubled cells refuse to die
environment.summary: Scientists have uncovered a surprising twist in how cells behave when division goes wrong. Sometimes a cell successfully copies its DNA but fails to split into two, leaving it with double the genetic material — a mistake linked to aging, cancer, and other major diseases. Researchers discovered that not all of these failures are equal.

mix_items.title: Cele mai apreciate titluri Alice Books la Bookfest 2026
mix_items.summary: De la poezie și ficțiune literară până la romane premiate și bestsellere internaționale, titlurile Alice Books au atras constant interesul cititorilor pe parcursul Bookfest 2026. mix_items.summary: Niciun animal nu rămâne prea multă vreme în libertate în Pipera! Mistreţul care a stârnit panică şi RO-Alerturi aseară a fost capturat, a trecut interviul şi e încadrat acum ca Junior Pig Officer la o companie multinaţională, anunţă nea Vasile, portarul clădirii de birouri lângă care s-au întâmplat evenimentele. „Fuga, fuga, prin porumb, ştiţi şi […] The post Mistreţul văzut ieri în Pipera a fost capturat şi acum e Junior Pig Officer la o multinaţională appeared first on Times New Roman .

mix_items.title: 😂 Cine a zis că n-au dinţi? Suveraniştii din Piaţa Victoriei au lăsat în urmă 2 tone de coji de seminţe
mix_items.summary: Propaganda soroşistă spune despre suveranişti că sunt doar nişte fascişti fără dinţi, dar te-ai gândit că poate minte de îngheaţă apele? Aurel Tetraşcu, medic stomatolog, susţine că are dovezi că suveraniştii sunt de fapt nişte fascişti cu dantura impecabilă. „Mă plimbam prin Piaţa Victoriei după ploaie şi am remarcat un detaliu incredibil: 200 de oameni […] The post Cine a zis că n-au dinţi? Suveraniştii din Piaţa Victoriei au lăsat în urmă 2 tone de coji de seminţe appeared first on Times New Roman .

mix_items.title: Un copil de 7 ani a fost luat pe sus de tată dintr-o școală din Sălaj, după o neînțelegere cu fosta soție
mix_items.summary: Au fost momente tensionate într-o școală din Sălaj: un bărbat de 59 de ani, cetățean italian, a intrat într-o sală de clasă pentru a-și vedea fiul în vârstă de 7 ani. Potrivit martorilor, acesta l-ar fi luat în brațe pe copil și ar fi ieșit în grabă din clasă, provocând panică în rândul profesorilor.

mix_items.title: Pentru Fundația Motivation, duminica trecută a fost despre fapte bune și, mai ales, despre o echipă unită în jurul unui scop dedicat binelui.
mix_items.summary: #TeamMotivation a alergat la Semimaraton Galați ca să schimbe în bine viețile copiilor și adulților cu dizabilități care au nevoie de sprijin în încercarea lor de a-și recăpăta independența. Evenimentul organizat de Fundația Comunitară Galați a adunat ambasadori a zeci de cauze pentru care alergătorii participanți au făcut tot posibilul să ajungă la finish. Pe lângă emoțiile și încurajările care au condimentat cursele din cadrul maratonului, pentru Fundația Motivation evenimentul a arătat așa: 14 utilizatori de scaun rulant... The post Pentru Fundația Motivation, duminica trecută a fost despre fapte bune și, mai ales, despre o echipă unită în jurul unui scop dedicat binelui. appeared first on Fundatia Motivation Romania .

mix_items.title: High blood pressure associated with lower risk of dementia in frail people
mix_items.summary: For people with physical frailty, having high blood pressure may be associated with a lower risk of dementia, according to a study published in Neurology. The study did not find a lower risk of dementia in people with high blood pressure who were more robust. High blood pressure was associated with a higher risk of dementia in people with no signs of frailty.

mix_items.title: Povestea Alexandrei – Un pas spre încredere, un pas spre viitor
mix_items.summary: Alexandra, adolescentă sprijinită de Fundația Inocenți, și-a recăpătat încrederea în sine și motivația pentru învățare prin suport educațional și emoțional. The post Povestea Alexandrei – Un pas spre încredere, un pas spre viitor appeared first on Fundația Inocenți .

mix_items.title: Fewer than 1 in 4 patients with stroke and 1 in 7 with brain injury receive inpatient rehab, finds study
mix_items.summary: Fewer than one in four people with stroke and fewer than one in seven people with traumatic brain injury receive inpatient rehabilitation care after being hospitalized, according to a study published in Neurology Open Access. Researchers found disparities in access across sex, race, insurance coverage and income level.

mix_items.title: How anti-CRISPR proteins promote the spread of hospital-acquired infections
mix_items.summary: Researchers from Skoltech—a VEB.RF group institution—and their colleagues from the U.S. and China have explained how the antibiotic resistance gene established itself in the genome of the bacterium Klebsiella pneumoniae. The findings could help control this widespread microbe, which can cause pneumonia, meningitis and other, often hospital-acquired, infections in patients with weakened immunity. The findings were reported in the Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences.

mix_items.title: Când dorința de a face bine este mai puternică decât emoțiile: Tiana și dragostea ei pentru voluntariat
mix_items.summary: Tiana are 21 de ani și face voluntariat de aproximativ 3 ani în programele și activitățile World Vision România. A crescut... Articolul Când dorința de a face bine este mai puternică decât emoțiile: Tiana și dragostea ei pentru voluntariat apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Report: ICE surges have triggered massive job losses—including among Americans
mix_items.summary: Since January 2025, the Trump administration has ramped up immigration enforcement. A key rationale for the policy is that it will open up jobs for Americans by reducing competition from undocumented immigrants.

mix_items.title: Schimbările mari încep cu pași mici: povestea lui Robert și drumul lui către advocacy la nivel internațional
mix_items.summary: Robert are 18 ani și este reprezentant al copiilor în Consiliul Consultativ al Copiilor și Tinerilor din cadrul World Vision România. Prin... Articolul Schimbările mari încep cu pași mici: povestea lui Robert și drumul lui către advocacy la nivel internațional apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: AI model 'hears' Bryde's whale calls in seismic data from South China Sea
mix_items.summary: Researchers have repurposed an AI model designed for visual identification tasks to detect Bryde's whale calls contained within seismic data collected in the South China Sea. The detection system precisely identified calls more than 96% of the time and even identified some call events that were missed when the data were examined by hand, according to the study published in Seismological Research Letters.

mix_items.title: Visul lui Alexandru începe cu o minge
mix_items.summary: Alexandru are 10 ani, locuiește într-un sat din județul Giurgiu și este elev în clasa a III‑a. Este un copil cu... Articolul Visul lui Alexandru începe cu o minge apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Hidden muscle resistance in advanced dementia is involuntary, not refusal, new papers explain
mix_items.summary: Baycrest researchers and clinicians have contributed to newly published research advancing understanding of paratonia, a common but often underrecognized condition affecting people living with advanced dementia.

mix_items.title: Everyone wants to think they're open‑minded. Here's why most people aren't
mix_items.summary: Most people think they are open-minded and would like others to perceive them as such. But for the things that matter most—religious beliefs, for example, or the meaning of life—few of us are genuinely willing to consider that we might be wrong, let alone do the hard work of revising beliefs. At a fundamental level, we humans are somewhat closed-minded.

mix_items.title: Sleep and exercise may curb heart risk from mutant white blood cells
mix_items.summary: Healthy sleep and regular exercise can work to counteract genetic mutations in white blood cells that are associated with cardiovascular disease and are most common among older people, Mount Sinai researchers have found. In a study published in Nature, the team reported for the first time that sufficient sleep and exercise can help reduce the cancer-like cell expansion and atherosclerotic risk linked to mutations that spontaneously occur in white blood cells.

mix_items.title: How ice-age sea-level falls may have turned seafloor volcanoes into ocean fertilizer
mix_items.summary: Ice-age sea-level declines may have turned seafloor volcanoes into natural iron fertilizer for plankton, potentially enhancing ocean carbon storage, Boston College researchers report in the journal Nature Geoscience.

mix_items.title: Think exercise can undo the effects of sitting all day? You may want to stand for this, study suggests
mix_items.summary: A new Dalhousie study suggests improved fitness may not be enough to protect blood vessels from the effects of prolonged sitting. The study, published in the European Journal of Applied Physiology, examined whether 12 weeks of high-intensity interval training could protect blood-vessel function in the leg after two hours of uninterrupted sitting.

mix_items.title: 'Cool Routes' finds cooler walking paths with hourly forecasts and street-level shade data
mix_items.summary: The Arizona sunshine hits like a blowtorch. The pavement radiates heat like a stove burner. To make hot-weather walking less of an ordeal, Arizona State University researchers have created a web-based app that finds the coolest, shadiest ways to reach destinations on foot.

mix_items.title: Dads today talk more freely with their teens about sex and relationships
mix_items.summary: For many dads, talking with their teenager about sex and relationships can feel like a minefield.

mix_items.title: Bilingual brains keep concepts aligned across languages, individual neuron data suggest
mix_items.summary: Researchers at Baylor College of Medicine have uncovered a fundamental principle underlying how the human brain processes meaning across multiple languages. In a new study posted to the bioRxiv preprint server, scientists recorded the activity of individual neurons in the human hippocampus and found that bilingual people appear to organize concepts in a shared neural structure—even when speaking entirely different languages.

mix_items.title: Can virtual reality train surgeons? A 2,000-year-old experiment reveals what's missing
mix_items.summary: As medical schools increasingly turn to virtual reality, 3D models and digital simulations, a new correspondence in Nature Medicine argues that one essential part of clinical training remains difficult to digitize: the hands-on judgment physicians develop through real-life, mentored practice.

mix_items.title: The Milky Way was rewired by a cataclysmic collision billions of years ago. Now it is on course for another
mix_items.summary: No matter the time or vantage point, from a pre-Neolithic cave to a post-lockdown London high-rise, the predictability of the night sky has always been humanity's symbol of permanence and reassuring stability.

mix_items.title: 'Black hole stars'—Webb finds strongest evidence yet
mix_items.summary: The complex puzzle known as little red dots has become more complete since their initial discovery by NASA's James Webb Space Telescope in 2022. Now a particular little red dot's spectrum is helping connect many of the pieces.

mix_items.title: Farmers in a national park are turning down lights at night to help wildlife. It could be good for crops too
mix_items.summary: Growing evidence suggests that excessive outdoor lighting at night may be harming wildlife.

mix_items.title: Sleep gets price tag in new survey of 455 US adults
mix_items.summary: A new study to be presented at the SLEEP 2026 annual meeting found that people place different financial values on sleep, with those differences linked to factors such as age, income and existing attitudes about sleep.

mix_items.title: 'Atmospheric scrubbing' could reduce cooling effects of stratospheric aerosol injections
mix_items.summary: The quest to identify a new way to potentially counter one of the world's most widely discussed solar geoengineering proposals has taken a new, exciting turn—raising questions about how future climate interventions could be governed.

mix_items.title: DNA tetrahedrons unlock sharper cancer targeting with vitamin E tweak
mix_items.summary: Conventional cancer treatments, such as chemotherapy, often lack specificity and can damage both cancerous and healthy cells, leading to severe side effects. With this in mind, researchers at Indian Institute of Technology Gandhinagar (IITGN) have developed DNA nanostructures called tetrahedrons and modified them by attaching a vitamin E-derived molecule called alpha-tocopherol succinate (αT), which can disrupt vital functions inside cancer cells while acting protectively in healthy cells. By incorporating αT into the DNA tetrahedrons, the researchers significantly enhanced cellular uptake and improved anticancer efficacy, resulting in more selective and effective elimination of cancer cells.

mix_items.title: ACS Geriatric Surgery Verification hospitals screen nearly all older surgical patients for delirium, study finds
mix_items.summary: There is a stark gap in how often hospitals screen older adults for postoperative delirium, with verified geriatric surgery programs screening nearly every patient while nonaccredited programs screen just half, according to new findings published in the Journal of the American College of Surgeons. This disparity may mean thousands of cases are going undetected and unaddressed.

mix_items.title: NASA, NOAA to Hold Joint Session at 23rd Symposium on Operational Environmental Satellite Systems
mix_items.summary: Abstracts are now being accepted for the session, which will take place at the 2027 AMS Annual Meeting.

mix_items.title: Expecting poor sleep is associated with worse postpartum sleep quality
mix_items.summary: A new study to be presented at the SLEEP 2026 annual meeting found that pregnant women's expectations about postpartum sleep predicted their actual sleep quality after delivery, outweighing prior sleep and psychiatric history.

mix_items.title: More sustainable agriculture: Recycled fertilizers could be part of the solution
mix_items.summary: Researchers have uncovered new insights into how phosphorus from recycled materials moves through soil—offering guidance to support more sustainable fertilizer use. Phosphorus is an essential nutrient for plant growth, yet many soils lack enough of it to support strong crop yields. Farmers often rely on fertilizers made from mined phosphorus, a limited, nonrenewable resource.

mix_items.title: Early Rett syndrome clues emerge as 12 genes shift before symptoms appear
mix_items.summary: To better understand what drives the emergence of symptoms in Rett syndrome, researchers at Baylor College of Medicine and the Duncan Neurological Research Institute (Duncan NRI) at Texas Children's Hospital took a closer look at brain cells in mice modeling Rett syndrome before symptoms appeared. They identified a set of dysfunctional genes and specific cell types that are vulnerable early to genetic changes. The study appears in Science Advances.

mix_items.title: Ultrasensitive blood test predicts head and neck cancer relapse months earlier
mix_items.summary: A new study by investigators from Mass General Brigham Cancer Institute has found that an ultrasensitive blood test called HPV-DeepSeek could help identify which people with HPV-associated head and neck cancer still had cancer cells in their bodies after surgery and may benefit the most from additional treatments. The results are published in Science Translational Medicine.

mix_items.title: Experts say ‘bare bones’ US laws are unfit to regulate nascent deep-sea mining industry
mix_items.summary: This is part 2 of a two-part series examining the U.S.’s efforts to begin deep-sea mining in federal waters. Part 2 examines the regulations that would govern the industry. Part 1 explored the process behind proposed lease sales in U.S. federal waters and reactions to those plans. The deep-sea mining industry could launch in the […]

mix_items.title: Colombia passes landmark cattle traceability law to combat illegal deforestation
mix_items.summary: Colombia passed a landmark law June 4 aimed at improving traceability of its cattle supply chain to ensure beef isn’t sourced from deforested land. The law hopes to enhance existing traceability systems and make it easier to identify when cattle have grazed in protected areas and forests that were illegally cleared for pasture. “This is […]

mix_items.title: Kenya’s former Chief Justice David Maraga arrested at protest of national park construction
mix_items.summary: NAIROBI, Kenya (AP) — Kenya’s former Chief Justice David Maraga said he was arrested Monday alongside other activists protesting planned construction inside Nairobi National Park. Police fired tear gas canisters at the protesters who were marching outside the park while carrying banners with messages denouncing land grabs. Maraga was detained and later released while staging […]

mix_items.title: Urban wildlife is changing from the inside out (commentary)
mix_items.summary: Cities are expanding faster than at any point in human history, and wildlife is adapting in remarkable ways. We often talk about visible changes like animals becoming bolder, shifting their diets, or altering their daily rhythms to avoid people. But there is a deeper transformation happening inside their bodies, one that conservation science has barely […]

mix_items.title: Scientists discover vast hidden structure beneath Antarctica’s ice
mix_items.summary: A giant fan-shaped network of hidden basins has been discovered beneath East Antarctica, revealing that several well-known subglacial features are actually part of one massive geological structure. The finding sheds new light on Antarctica’s ancient tectonic history and could help scientists better understand how the ice sheet behaves today.

mix_items.title: This prehistoric fish may explain how animals first walked on Earth
mix_items.summary: Scientists have peered inside the skull of a 380-million-year-old Antarctic fish that was closely related to the first animals to walk on land, revealing surprising clues about how life began its move out of the water. Using advanced neutron imaging, researchers discovered that Koharalepis jarviki had features suited for living near the water’s surface, including openings in its skull that may have helped it gulp air and a light-sensitive organ linked to day-night rhythms.

mix_items.title: 100-million-year-old bug had crab-like claws unlike any known insect
mix_items.summary: Deep inside 100-million-year-old amber from Myanmar, scientists uncovered a bizarre ancient bug with clawed front legs that look more like a crab’s pincers than anything seen in modern insects. The discovery is so unusual that researchers say these crab-like “chelae” evolved independently in this lineage, making it only the fourth known example of such structures appearing in insects at all.

mix_items.title: Scientists discover why some DNA-doubled cells refuse to die
mix_items.summary: Scientists have uncovered a surprising twist in how cells behave when division goes wrong. Sometimes a cell successfully copies its DNA but fails to split into two, leaving it with double the genetic material — a mistake linked to aging, cancer, and other major diseases. Researchers discovered that not all of these failures are equal.

joke_ro.text: Nu sunt lenes. Sunt pe modul economie de energie.

satire_ro.title: Nu doar urşii sunt periculoşi! Turist ardelean, atacat fulgerător de o ţestoasă în Munţii Dobrogei
